title: Raynaud's - NHS
text:
not have too much caffeine (found in tea, coffee, cola and chocolate) – it may trigger the symptoms of Raynaud's
Non-urgent advice: See a GP if:
you have symptoms of Raynaud's that are very bad or getting worse Raynaud's is affecting your daily life your symptoms are only on 1 side of your body you also have joint pain, skin rashes or muscle weakness you're over 30 years old and get symptoms of Raynaud's for the first time your child is under 12 years old and has symptoms of Raynaud's 
Treatment for Raynaud's from a GP 
If you have Raynaud's and your symptoms are very bad or getting worse, a GP may prescribe a medicine to help improve your circulation. For example, you may be offered nifedipine , which is used to treat high blood pressure.
Some people need to take this medicine every day. Others only use it to prevent Raynaud's, for example, during cold weather.
A GP may arrange tests if they think Raynaud's could be a sign of a more serious condition, such as rheumatoid arthritis or lupus .
Information: 
Support from Scleroderma and Raynaud's UK (SRUK) SRUK is a charity for people with scleroderma or Raynaud's.
SRUK: further information and advice about living with Raynaud's SRUK: information on how to find support groups
